content:
# Luke Chapter 18 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 110-111 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_018_page_001.jpg, LUK_018_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And he was telling them a parable, to the end that they ought always to pray and not to lose heart,

**2** saying, "There was a certain judge in a certain city who did not fear God and did not regard man.

**3** And there was a widow in that city, and she kept coming to him, saying, 'Avenge me of my adversary.'

**4** And for a time he was not willing; but after these things he said within himself, 'Even though I do not fear God nor regard man,

**5** yet because this widow causes me trouble, I will avenge her, lest by her continual coming she wear me out.'"

**6** And the Lord said, "Hear what the unrighteous judge says.

**7** And shall not God bring about the avenging of his chosen ones, who cry out to him day and night, and is he patient over them?

**8** I say to you that he will bring about their avenging quickly. Nevertheless, when the Son of Man comes, will he find faith upon the earth?"

**9** And he spoke also this parable to certain ones who trusted in themselves that they were righteous and despised the rest:

**10** "Two men went up into the temple to pray, the one a Pharisee and the other a tax collector.

**11** The Pharisee, having stood, was praying these things to himself: 'God, I thank you that I am not as the rest of men — extortioners, unrighteous, adulterers — or even as this tax collector.

**12** I fast twice in the week; I give tithes of all that I acquire.'

**13** But the tax collector, standing far off, would not even lift up his eyes to heaven, but was striking his breast, saying, 'God, be merciful to me, the sinner.'

**14** I say to you, this one went down to his house justified rather than that one. For everyone who exalts himself shall be humbled, and he who humbles himself shall be exalted."

**15** And they were bringing to him also the infants, that he might touch them; but the disciples, having seen it, rebuked them.

**16** But Jesus called them to himself, saying, "Permit the children to come to me, and do not forbid them, for of such is the kingdom of God.

**17** Truly I say to you, whoever does not receive the kingdom of God as a child shall by no means enter into it."

**18** And a certain ruler asked him, saying, "Good Teacher, what shall I do to inherit eternal life?"

**19** And Jesus said to him, "Why do you call me good? No one is good except one — God.

**20** You know the commandments: 'Do not commit adultery, do not murder, do not steal, do not bear false witness, honor your father and mother.'"

**21** And he said, "All these things I have kept from my youth."

**22** And having heard this, Jesus said to him, "Yet one thing is lacking to you: sell all that you have and distribute to the poor, and you shall have treasure in heaven; and come, follow me."

**23** But having heard these things, he became exceedingly sorrowful, for he was very wealthy.

**24** And Jesus, seeing him, said, "How difficultly shall those who have riches enter into the kingdom of God!

**25** For it is easier for a camel to enter through the eye of a needle than for a rich man to enter into the kingdom of God."

**26** And those who heard it said, "And who is able to be saved?"

**27** And he said, "The things impossible with men are possible with God."

**28** And Peter said, "Behold, we have left our own things and followed you."

**29** And he said to them, "Truly I say to you, there is no one who has left house or wife or brothers or parents or children for the sake of the kingdom of God,

**30** who shall not receive manifold more in this time, and in the age to come, eternal life."

**31** And having taken the twelve aside, he said to them, "Behold, we are going up to Jerusalem, and all the things that have been written through the prophets concerning the Son of Man shall be accomplished.

**32** For he shall be delivered over to the Gentiles, and shall be mocked, and shall be insulted, and shall be spat upon,

**33** and having scourged him, they shall kill him; and on the third day he shall rise."

**34** And they understood none of these things, and this saying was hidden from them, and they did not comprehend the things being said.

**35** And it came to pass, as he drew near to Jericho, a certain blind man was sitting beside the road, begging.

**36** And having heard a crowd passing through, he inquired what this might be.

**37** And they reported to him that Jesus the Nazarene is passing by.

**38** And he cried out, saying, "Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me!"

**39** And those who were going before rebuked him, that he should be silent; but he cried out much more, "Son of David, have mercy on me!"

**40** And Jesus, having stood still, commanded him to be brought to him. And when he had drawn near, he asked him,

**41** "What do you wish that I should do for you?" And he said, "Lord, that I may receive my sight."

**42** And Jesus said to him, "Receive your sight; your faith has saved you."

**43** And immediately he received his sight and was following him, glorifying God. And all the people, having seen it, gave praise to God.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Verification

The two Sinaiticus manuscript images (pages 110-111) were examined. Page 1 covers approximately Luke 17:26 through Luke 18:14 across four columns, with column markers at Lk. 17:26, Lk. 17:35, Lk. 18:7, and Lk. 18:14. Page 2 covers approximately Luke 18:22 through Luke 19:5 across four columns, with column markers at Lk. 18:22, Lk. 18:30, Lk. 18:39, and Lk. 19:5. The text is in Sinaiticus's characteristic continuous uncial script, four columns per page. Key readings confirmed from the manuscript:

- ΠΡΟΣ ΤΟ ΔΕΙΝ ΠΑΝΤΟΤΕ ΠΡΟΣΕΥΧΕΣΘΑΙ ΑΥΤΟΥΣ ΚΑΙ ΜΗ ΕΝΚΑΚΕΙΝ (18:1, "that they ought always to pray and not to lose heart")
- ΚΡΙΤΗΣ ΤΙΣ ΗΝ ΕΝ ΤΙΝΙ ΠΟΛΕΙ ΤΟΝ ΘΕΟΝ ΜΗ ΦΟΒΟΥΜΕΝΟΣ (18:2, "a certain judge was in a certain city, not fearing God")
- ΕΚΔΙΚΗΣΟΝ ΜΕ ΑΠΟ ΤΟΥ ΑΝΤΙΔΙΚΟΥ ΜΟΥ (18:3, "avenge me of my adversary")
- Ο ΚΡΙΤΗΣ ΤΗΣ ΑΔΙΚΙΑΣ (18:6, "the judge of unrighteousness/the unrighteous judge")
- ΤΩΝ ΕΚΛΕΚΤΩΝ ΑΥΤΟΥ (18:7, "his chosen ones/elect")
- ΑΡΑ ΕΥΡΗΣΕΙ ΤΗΝ ΠΙΣΤΙΝ ΕΠΙ ΤΗΣ ΓΗΣ (18:8, "will he find faith upon the earth")
- ΤΟΥΣ ΠΕΠΟΙΘΟΤΑΣ ΕΦ ΕΑΥΤΟΙΣ ΟΤΙ ΕΙΣΙΝ ΔΙΚΑΙΟΙ (18:9, "who trusted in themselves that they were righteous")
- ΣΤΑΘΕΙΣ ΤΑΥΤΑ ΠΡΟΣ ΕΑΥΤΟΝ ΠΡΟΣΗΥΧΕΤΟ (18:11, "having stood, was praying these things to himself")
- Ο ΘΕΟΣ ΙΛΑΣΘΗΤΙ ΜΟΙ ΤΩ ΑΜΑΡΤΩΛΩ (18:13, "God, be merciful to me, the sinner")
- ΔΕΔΙΚΑΙΩΜΕΝΟΣ (18:14, "having been justified/justified")
- ΤΑ ΒΡΕΦΗ (18:15, "the infants")
- ΔΙΔΑΣΚΑΛΕ ΑΓΑΘΕ (18:18, "Good Teacher")
- ΕΤΙ ΕΝ ΣΟΙ ΛΕΙΠΕΙ (18:22, "yet one thing is lacking to you")
- ΠΕΡΙΛΥΠΟΣ ΕΓΕΝΗΘΗ (18:23, "he became exceedingly sorrowful")
- ΚΑΜΗΛΟΝ ΔΙΑ ΤΡΗΜΑΤΟΣ ΒΕΛΟΝΗΣ (18:25, "a camel through the eye of a needle")
- ΤΑ ΑΔΥΝΑΤΑ ΠΑΡΑ ΑΝΘΡΩΠΟΙΣ ΔΥΝΑΤΑ ΠΑΡΑ ΤΩ ΘΕΩ ΕΣΤΙΝ (18:27, "the things impossible with men are possible with God")
- ΗΜΕΙΣ ΑΦΕΝΤΕΣ ΤΑ ΙΔΙΑ (18:28, "we have left our own things")
- ΠΟΛΛΑΠΛΑΣΙΟΝΑ (18:30, "manifold more")
- ΤΕΛΕΣΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ ΠΑΝΤΑ ΤΑ ΓΕΓΡΑΜΜΕΝΑ ΔΙΑ ΤΩΝ ΠΡΟΦΗΤΩΝ (18:31, "all the things written through the prophets shall be accomplished")
- ΟΥΔΕΝ ΤΟΥΤΩΝ ΣΥΝΗΚΑΝ ... ΚΕΚΡΥΜΜΕΝΟΝ ΑΠ ΑΥΤΩΝ (18:34, "they understood none of these things ... hidden from them")
- ΕΝ ΤΩ ΕΓΓΙΖΕΙΝ ΑΥΤΟΝ ΕΙΣ ΙΕΡΙΧΩ (18:35, "as he drew near to Jericho")
- ΙΗΣΟΥ ΥΙΕΔΑΥΕΙΔ ΕΛΕΗΣΟΝ ΜΕ (18:38, "Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me")
- Η ΠΙΣΤΙΣ ΣΟΥ ΣΕΣΩΚΕΝ ΣΕ (18:42, "your faith has saved you")

### Translation Principles Applied

1. **18:1 — "he was telling them a parable" vs. "he was speaking a parable to them" vs. "he told them a parable":** EMET renders "he was speaking a parable to them," LOGOS "he was telling them a parable," PNEUMA "Jesus told them a parable." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "was telling" preserves the imperfect tense (ΕΛΕΓΕΝ) indicating ongoing discourse, and the phrasing is natural English; PNEUMA's aorist-like "told" loses the imperfect force; EMET's "speaking a parable" is slightly less idiomatic than "telling a parable."

2. **18:1 — "to the end that they ought always to pray" vs. "about the [need] to always pray":** EMET renders "about the [need] to always pray," LOGOS "to the end that they ought always to pray," PNEUMA "about their need to pray at all times." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "to the end that" renders ΠΡΟΣ ΤΟ purposively, and "they ought" captures ΔΕΙΝ; EMET brackets "[need]" as a supplement; PNEUMA paraphrases freely.

3. **18:2 — "who did not fear God and did not regard man" vs. "not fearing God and not having-regard-for man":** EMET renders "not fearing God and not having-regard-for man," LOGOS "who did not fear God and did not regard man," PNEUMA "who had no fear of God and no respect for people." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — the relative clause construction is natural English while preserving the participial characterization; EMET's participial form is over-literal; PNEUMA's "no respect for people" replaces the singular ΑΝΘΡΩΠΟΝ with a plural.

4. **18:5 — "lest by her continual coming she wear me out" vs. "lest coming to-the-end she wear-me-out":** EMET renders "lest coming to-the-end she wear-me-out," LOGOS "lest by her continual coming she wear me out," PNEUMA "otherwise she will keep coming and exhaust me completely." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "by her continual coming" renders ΕΙΣ ΤΕΛΟΣ ΕΡΧΟΜΕΝΗ naturally; EMET's "coming to-the-end" is awkwardly literal; PNEUMA paraphrases.

5. **18:6 — "the unrighteous judge" vs. "the judge of unrighteousness":** EMET renders "the judge of unrighteousness," LOGOS "the unrighteous judge," PNEUMA "the unjust judge." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "the unrighteous judge" renders the Hebraic genitive of quality (Ο ΚΡΙΤΗΣ ΤΗΣ ΑΔΙΚΙΑΣ) as an English adjective-noun construction, preserving the sense while being natural; EMET's genitive form, while literal, is awkward; PNEUMA's "unjust" is acceptable but ΑΔΙΚΙΑ maps more precisely to "unrighteousness."

6. **18:7 — "bring about the avenging" vs. "accomplish the avenging" vs. "bring about justice":** EMET renders "bring-about the avenging," LOGOS "accomplish the avenging," PNEUMA "bring about justice." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS in retaining "avenging" (ΕΚΔΙΚΗΣΙΝ) rather than PNEUMA's "justice"; between the two, SOPHIA selects "bring about" (EMET) as more natural than "accomplish" (LOGOS) for ΠΟΙΗΣΗ.

7. **18:7 — "chosen ones" vs. "elect":** EMET renders "chosen-ones," LOGOS "elect," PNEUMA "chosen ones." SOPHIA follows EMET and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "chosen ones" renders ΕΚΛΕΚΤΩΝ accessibly while preserving the substantive force; "elect" (LOGOS) is more formal but less natural in modern English.

8. **18:8 — "quickly" vs. "speedily":** EMET renders "quickly," LOGOS "speedily," PNEUMA "quickly." SOPHIA follows EMET and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "quickly" renders ΕΝ ΤΑΧΕΙ naturally; "speedily" (LOGOS) is slightly archaic.

9. **18:8 — "when the Son of Man comes, will he find" vs. "the Son of Man, having come, will he find":** EMET renders "the Son of Man, having come, will he find," LOGOS "when the Son of Man comes, will he find," PNEUMA "when the Son of Man comes, will he find." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "when the Son of Man comes" renders ΕΛΘΩΝ as a temporal clause naturally in English; EMET's participial construction is over-literal.

10. **18:9 — "trusted in themselves" vs. "had-persuaded-themselves" vs. "were confident in their own righteousness":** EMET renders "had-persuaded-themselves," LOGOS "trusted in themselves," PNEUMA "were confident in their own righteousness." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "trusted in themselves" renders ΠΕΠΟΙΘΟΤΑΣ ΕΦ ΕΑΥΤΟΙΣ precisely; EMET's "had-persuaded-themselves" overtranslates; PNEUMA paraphrases.

11. **18:11 — "I thank you" vs. "I thank thee":** EMET renders "I thank you," LOGOS "I thank thee," PNEUMA "I thank you." SOPHIA follows EMET and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "you" is consistent with SOPHIA's practice of rendering second-person address in modern English.

12. **18:11 — "as the rest of men" vs. "like the rest of men":** EMET renders "like the rest of men," LOGOS "as the rest of men," PNEUMA "like the rest of people." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "as" renders ΩΣ more precisely than "like" in formal register; PNEUMA's "people" replaces ΑΝΘΡΩΠΩΝ unnecessarily.

13. **18:13 — "be merciful to me, the sinner" vs. "have mercy on me, a sinner":** EMET renders "be-merciful to me, the sinner," LOGOS "be merciful to me, the sinner," PNEUMA "have mercy on me, a sinner." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "be merciful" renders ΙΛΑΣΘΗΤΙ more precisely than "have mercy"; the article ΤΩ before ΑΜΑΡΤΩΛΩ requires "the sinner" not "a sinner" (PNEUMA).

14. **18:14 — "because" vs. "for":** EMET renders "because everyone who exalts himself," LOGOS "for everyone who exalts himself," PNEUMA "for everyone who exalts himself." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "for" renders ΟΤΙ in its causal sense with appropriate formal register; "because" (EMET) is acceptable but less elegant in this context.

15. **18:15 — "having seen it, rebuked them" vs. "having seen [this], were rebuking them":** EMET renders "having seen [this], were rebuking them," LOGOS "having seen it, rebuked them," PNEUMA "when the disciples saw this, they rebuked them." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "having seen it, rebuked them" is concise and natural; EMET's imperfect "were rebuking" may be more precise for ΕΠΕΤΙΜΩΝ but "rebuked" is acceptable; PNEUMA restructures the sentence.

16. **18:16 — "children" vs. "little-children":** EMET renders "little-children," LOGOS "children," PNEUMA "little children." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "children" renders ΠΑΙΔΙΑ without the diminutive addition; EMET hyphenates unnecessarily.

17. **18:16 — "do not forbid them" vs. "do not hinder them" vs. "do not try to stop them":** EMET renders "do not hinder them," LOGOS "do not forbid them," PNEUMA "do not try to stop them." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "forbid" renders ΚΩΛΥΕΤΕ precisely; EMET's "hinder" is acceptable; PNEUMA adds "try to" which is not in the Greek.

18. **18:18 — "what shall I do to inherit" vs. "what having done shall I inherit" vs. "what must I do to inherit":** EMET renders "what having done shall I inherit," LOGOS "what shall I do to inherit," PNEUMA "what must I do to inherit." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "what shall I do to inherit" renders ΤΙ ΠΟΙΗΣΑΣ ... ΚΛΗΡΟΝΟΜΗΣΩ naturally; EMET's participial form is over-literal; PNEUMA's "must" adds obligation not in the Greek.

19. **18:22 — "Yet one thing is lacking to you" vs. "Still one [thing] is lacking to you" vs. "There is still one thing you lack":** EMET renders "Still one [thing] is lacking to you," LOGOS "Yet one thing is lacking to you," PNEUMA "There is still one thing you lack." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "Yet" renders ΕΤΙ crisply; EMET brackets "[thing]" unnecessarily; PNEUMA restructures.

20. **18:23 — "very wealthy" vs. "very rich" vs. "extremely wealthy":** EMET renders "very wealthy," LOGOS "very rich," PNEUMA "extremely wealthy." SOPHIA follows EMET — "very wealthy" renders ΠΛΟΥΣΙΟΣ ΣΦΟΔΡΑ precisely; LOGOS's "rich" is acceptable; PNEUMA's "extremely" overtranslates ΣΦΟΔΡΑ slightly.

21. **18:24 — "seeing him" vs. "having seen him" vs. "looked at him":** EMET renders "having seen him," LOGOS "seeing him become exceedingly sorrowful," PNEUMA "looked at him." SOPHIA renders "seeing him" — a compromise: EMET's participle is preserved in sense; LOGOS adds detail ("become exceedingly sorrowful") not in all readings; PNEUMA restructures with a finite verb. The Sinaiticus reading ΙΔΩΝ ΑΥΤΟΝ supports "seeing him."

22. **18:25 — "enter through the eye of a needle" vs. "squeeze through the eye of a needle":** EMET renders "enter through [the] eye of a needle," LOGOS "enter through the eye of a needle," PNEUMA "squeeze through the eye of a needle." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "enter" renders ΕΙΣΕΛΘΕΙΝ literally; PNEUMA's "squeeze" is interpretive.

23. **18:28 — "our own things" vs. "everything we had":** EMET renders "our-own [things]," LOGOS "our own things," PNEUMA "everything we had." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "our own things" renders ΤΑ ΙΔΙΑ precisely; PNEUMA paraphrases.

24. **18:31 — "shall be accomplished" vs. "will be completed" vs. "will be fulfilled":** EMET renders "will be completed," LOGOS "shall be accomplished," PNEUMA "will be fulfilled." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "shall be accomplished" renders ΤΕΛΕΣΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ formally; PNEUMA's "fulfilled" is acceptable but ΤΕΛΕΩ maps more precisely to "accomplished/completed."

25. **18:32 — "Gentiles" vs. "nations":** EMET renders "nations," LOGOS "Gentiles," PNEUMA "Gentiles." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "Gentiles" renders ΕΘΝΕΣΙΝ in its contextual sense (non-Jewish peoples who will execute Jesus); EMET's "nations" is the generic rendering but "Gentiles" is more precise here.

26. **18:33 — "he shall rise" vs. "he will rise-again":** EMET renders "he will rise-again," LOGOS "he shall rise," PNEUMA "he will rise again." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "he shall rise" renders ΑΝΑΣΤΗΣΕΤΑΙ concisely; the prefix ΑΝΑ- ("again") is implicit in "rise" in a resurrection context.

27. **18:34 — "this saying was hidden" vs. "this word was hidden":** EMET renders "this word was hidden," LOGOS "this saying was hidden," PNEUMA "the meaning of these words was hidden." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "saying" renders ΡΗΜΑ in narrative context appropriately; PNEUMA adds "meaning" which is not in the Greek.

28. **18:34 — "they did not comprehend" vs. "they were not knowing":** EMET renders "they were not knowing," LOGOS "they did not comprehend," PNEUMA "they did not grasp." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "did not comprehend" renders ΟΥΚ ΕΓΙΝΩΣΚΟΝ naturally; EMET's "were not knowing" is awkwardly literal; PNEUMA's "grasp" is acceptable but less formal.

29. **18:35 — "it came to pass, as he drew near" vs. "it came-to-be, in his drawing-near" vs. "as Jesus was approaching":** EMET renders "it came-to-be, in his drawing-near to Jericho," LOGOS "it came to pass, as he drew near to Jericho," PNEUMA "as Jesus was approaching Jericho." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — consistent with SOPHIA's established rendering of ΕΓΕΝΕΤΟ (cf. 17:11); EMET's construction is over-literal; PNEUMA omits ΕΓΕΝΕΤΟ entirely.

30. **18:39 — "those who were going before" vs. "those going-before" vs. "those at the front of the crowd":** EMET renders "those going-before," LOGOS "those who were going before," PNEUMA "those at the front of the crowd." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — the relative clause is natural English; PNEUMA adds "of the crowd" which is not in the Greek.

31. **18:41 — "that I may receive my sight" vs. "that I might receive-sight" vs. "I want to see again":** EMET renders "that I might receive-sight," LOGOS "that I may receive my sight," PNEUMA "I want to see again." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "that I may receive my sight" renders ΙΝΑ ΑΝΑΒΛΕΨΩ with the subjunctive appropriately; PNEUMA restructures entirely.

32. **18:42 — "has saved you" vs. "has healed you":** EMET renders "has saved you," LOGOS "has saved you," PNEUMA "has healed you." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "saved" preserves the full theological weight of ΣΕΣΩΚΕΝ, consistent with SOPHIA's rendering in 17:19; PNEUMA's "healed" reduces the verb to physical restoration.

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA, from Codex Sinaiticus (4th century CE).*
